title: Excess nitrogen from croplands
subtitle: "Excess nitrogen" is the difference between nutrient inputs (from fertilizers, manure, and fixation from legumes) and the amount harvested in crop material. This represents nitrogen that are lost to the environment and can create ecological imbalances on ecosystems and in water bodies.
caption:
Excess nitrogen from croplands

"Excess nitrogen" is the difference between nutrient inputs (from fertilizers, manure, and fixation from legumes) and the amount harvested in crop material. This represents nitrogen that are lost to the environment and can create ecological imbalances on ecosystems and in water bodies.

This choropleth world map displays 2009 data on excess nitrogen from croplands, measured in tonnes. The legend ranges from "Nitrogen mining" (shown in blue) through increasing quantities of excess nitrogen, with specific thresholds at 100,000 tonnes, 250,000 tonnes, 1 million tonnes, 10 million tonnes, and 25 million tonnes. The darkest brown shades, indicating values above 10 million tonnes, are visible in China, India, and the United States. Brazil and parts of Western Europe, including France and Germany, appear in the mid-range orange bands, falling between the 1 million and 10 million tonne thresholds. Canada, Australia, and Russia sit in lighter orange shades that correspond to the lower end of the excess nitrogen scale, generally between 100,000 and 1 million tonnes. A distinct blue color, labeled as "Nitrogen mining," covers large portions of Africa, including countries like Niger, Mali, and Botswana, as well as Kazakhstan and Mongolia. Greenland and several smaller island nations are hatched or pale, indicating no data is available. The visual distribution shows the highest absolute amounts of excess nitrogen concentrated in major agricultural economies in Asia and North America, while significant areas of Africa and Central Asia show nitrogen mining rather than excess.
